title: Concurrent Web Scraper
description: Write a program that fetches data from multiple websites simultaneously using goroutines and channels.

steps.title: The Sequential Scraper 🐌
steps.content:
Start by writing a simple function that fetches a URL and measures the time it takes. Use the `net/http` and `time` packages.

Create a function called `fetchSequential(url string)`:
1. Record the start time using `time.Now()`.
2. Make an `http.Get(url)` request.
3. Print the time elapsed when the request finishes.

Create a slice of 5 different testing URLs in your `main()` function and loop through them, calling `fetchSequential` on each.

steps.title: The Data Structure 📦
steps.content:
To make our concurrent fetcher robust, we should create a struct to hold our results before sending them over a channel. 

```go
type FetchResult struct {
    URL         string
    ElapsedTime time.Duration
    Error       error
}
```

steps.title: Unleash Goroutines 🚀
steps.content:
Now create a `fetchConcurrent(url string, ch chan<- FetchResult)` function.

It should do exactly what the sequential fetcher did, but instead of just printing the time elapsed, it creates a `FetchResult` struct and sends it into the `ch` channel.

*Important:* Don't forget to handle the `err` returned by `http.Get` internally by passing it into the struct.

steps.title: Channel Coordination 🎧
steps.content:
Back in your `main()` function, prepare for concurrency:

1. Initialize a channel: `ch := make(chan FetchResult)`.
2. Iterate over your slice of 5 testing URLs.
3. Instead of calling the fetcher directly, fire off a goroutine for each URL: `go fetchConcurrent(url, ch)`.

steps.title: Gather the Results 📥
Race to the Finish Link 🏁
steps.content:
Run both the sequential and concurrent fetchers in your `main()` function. Put a timer around the entire sequential block and the entire concurrent block.

You should see a massive performance gain! While the sequential block waits on each website, the concurrent version waits on them all simultaneously!
